item.title: Zircon and apatite geochemical and isotopic constraints on magma fertility and magmatic evolution of porphyry CuAu mineralization at the Ting Darigun prospect, Chagai Belt, Western Pakistan
item.dc:creator: Muhammad Hafeez
Xiaoyong Yang
Ahmed Shah
Zahra Abbasi
Salman Ahmed Khattak
Ibrar Khan
item.description: The Chagai magmatic Belt in western Pakistan hosts a major porphyry Cu Au province within the eastern Tethyan domain; yet the petrogenesis and fertility evolution of its central magmatic units remain poorly constrained. This study presents an integrated geochemical and geochronological investigation of the newly discovered Ting Darigun porphyry prospect, combining zircon U Pb dating, whole-rock geochemistry, Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic analyses and accessory mineral chemistry. New zircon U Pb ages indicate emplacement of pre-ore diorite in the Late Cretaceous (94.5 ± 1.9 Ma), followed by fertile granodiorite and coeval mineralized andesite porphyries in the Late Oligocene (25.45 ± 0.71 Ma and 25.05 ± 0.74 Ma, respectively). The pre-ore diorite records relatively dry, reduced arc magmatism derived from a depleted mantle source during an early immature arc stage, whereas the Late Oligocene porphyries exhibit adakitic characteristics, relatively higher initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr ratios, and positive εNd(t) and εHf(t) values, indicating that they were derived from hydrous and oxidized magmas influenced by progressive slab-derived metasomatism and enhanced crust–mantle interaction during arc maturation. Integrated whole-rock, zircon, and apatite geochemistry further indicate elevated magmatic water content and oxygen fugacity in the mineralized porphyries, which promoted amphibole-dominated differentiation and suppressed early plagioclase fractionation, thereby enhancing the development of a fertile magmatic–hydrothermal system responsible for Cu Au mineralization. Collectively, these results demonstrate that progressive tectono-magmatic evolution during the Late Oligocene fundamentally enhanced magma fertility in the central Chagai arc, providing new constraints on the temporal development of fertile porphyry systems in evolving continental arcs. Published: 2026-06-01T07:54:55+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.676 (must_read)

The Chagai magmatic Belt in western Pakistan hosts a major porphyry Cu Au province within the eastern Tethyan domain; yet the petrogenesis and fertility evolution of its central magmatic units remain poorly constrained. This study presents an integrated geochemical and geochronological investigation of the newly discovered Ting Darigun porphyry prospect, combining zircon U Pb dating, whole-rock geochemistry, Sr–Nd–Hf isotopic analyses and accessory mineral chemistry. New zircon U Pb ages indicate emplacement of pre-ore diorite in the Late Cretaceous (94.5 ± 1.9 Ma), followed by fertile granodiorite and coeval mineralized andesite porphyries in the Late Oligocene (25.45 ± 0.71 Ma and 25.05 ± 0.74 Ma, respectively). The pre-ore diorite records relatively dry, reduced arc magmatism derived from a depleted mantle source during an early immature arc stage, whereas the Late Oligocene porphyries exhibit adakitic characteristics, relatively higher initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr ratios, and positive εNd(t) and εHf(t) values, indicating that they were derived from hydrous and oxidized magmas influenced by progressive slab-derived metasomatism and enhanced crust–mantle interaction during arc maturation. Integrated whole-rock, zircon, and apatite geochemistry further indicate elevated magmatic water content and oxygen fugacity in the mineralized porphyries, which promoted amphibole-dominated differentiation and suppressed early plagioclase fractionation, thereby enhancing the development of a fertile magmatic–hydrothermal system responsible for Cu Au mineralization. Collectively, these results demonstrate that progressive tectono-magmatic evolution during the Late Oligocene fundamentally enhanced magma fertility in the central Chagai arc, providing new constraints on the temporal development of fertile porphyry systems in evolving continental arcs.

item.title: Increased Cu fertility from Paleocene Lurige to Eocene Narigongma porphyries in the northern Yulong belt, eastern Tibet: Implication for coupled Plateau uplift and porphyry Cu mineralization
item.dc:creator: Si-Lan Liu
Ming-Liang Huang
Xian-Wu Bi
Lei-Luo Xu
Jing-Jing Zhu
Wei Gao
Shan-Bin Bao
Lin-Bo Shang
item.description: The Yulong belt in eastern Tibet is a typical post-subduction porphyry Cu belt. Large to giant Cu deposits are widely developed in middle to south section of the belt, whereas only two small to medium deposits (Lurige and Narigongma) are currently discovered in the northern section. The petrogenesis and Cu fertility of porphyries from the northern section remain poorly investigated. Here we address these issues by presenting comprehensive whole-rock and mineral analyses on porphyries from these two deposits. The Lurige (∼58 Ma) and Narigongma (∼40 Ma) porphyries have high whole-rock SiO 2 (> 68 wt%) contents and overlapped Sr-Nd-Hf isotopes, which plot in the evolution trend of cospatial Permian Paleo-Tethyan arc magmas, suggesting their derivation from Paleo-Tethyan arc root. The two deposits coincide broadly with the two episodes of regional plateau uplift at ∼ 64–50 Ma and 44–40 Ma, indicating that regional plateau uplift played a first-order role in porphyry Cu mineralization. Published: 2026-05-30T05:24:45+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.654 (must_read)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107356

Relevance: 0.654 (must_read)

The Yulong belt in eastern Tibet is a typical post-subduction porphyry Cu belt. Large to giant Cu deposits are widely developed in middle to south section of the belt, whereas only two small to medium deposits (Lurige and Narigongma) are currently discovered in the northern section. The petrogenesis and Cu fertility of porphyries from the northern section remain poorly investigated. Here we address these issues by presenting comprehensive whole-rock and mineral analyses on porphyries from these two deposits. The Lurige (∼58 Ma) and Narigongma (∼40 Ma) porphyries have high whole-rock SiO 2 (> 68 wt%) contents and overlapped Sr-Nd-Hf isotopes, which plot in the evolution trend of cospatial Permian Paleo-Tethyan arc magmas, suggesting their derivation from Paleo-Tethyan arc root. The two deposits coincide broadly with the two episodes of regional plateau uplift at ∼ 64–50 Ma and 44–40 Ma, indicating that regional plateau uplift played a first-order role in porphyry Cu mineralization.

item.title: A Late Carboniferous slab window driving continental crustal growth and gold enrichment in the western Tajik South Tianshan, SW Altaids
item.dc:creator: He Yang
Wenjiao Xiao
Miao Sang
Yunus Mamadjanov
Dzhovid Yogibekov
Jingmin Gan
Heng Li
Wei Zhao
item.description: The South Tianshan, stretching for ∼2500 km, marks the orogenic termination of the southwestern Altaids, with considerable continental growth and gold mineralization. However, despite extensive study, its tectonic nature and detailed evolutionary history remain controversial, and their link to continental growth and gold mineralization are insufficiently constrained. Here, we present a systematic study of the Late Carboniferous quartz diorite porphyrites, granodiorite porphyries and monzogranites from the Chore gold field and adjacent areas in the Tajik South Tianshan. LA–ICP–MS and LA–MC–ICP–MS zircon U–Pb dating of these intrusions yields magma crystallization ages of ∼308–300 Ma. The quartz diorite porphyrites and granodiorite porphyries are adakitic, with high Na 2 O (4.59–5.18 wt%), high Sr/Y (61–78) and relatively depleted isotopic compositions (e.g., ε Nd ( t ) = +0.1 to +1.3), indicating derivation from partial melting of the subducting Turkestan oceanic slab (MORB + minor overlying sediment). Minor pyrite occurs within the localized areas of the adakitic intrusions, which contains up to 1.76 ppm Au, indicating associated gold enrichment. The monzogranites are high-K calc-alkaline rocks that exhibit greater compositional variability. They possess enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotopes and slightly high δ 66 Zn values (ca. +0.35‰), consistent with melting of a quartzofeldspathic crustal source followed by mixing with minor mantle-derived magmas. The discovery of slab-derived adakitic rocks supports the view that the South Tianshan consists mainly of accretionary complex. Based on available data, we suggest that nearly trench-orthogonal tearing of the subducting Turkestan oceanic lithosphere, accompanied by slab window formation, could have occurred at ∼308–300 Ma. This process induced partial melting of both slab and crustal materials, generated a southward younging magmatic trend, and drove gold enrichment in the region. Additionally, we propose that slab melting, mantle-derived magma intrusion and the accretion of mantle-derived rocks are important ways to continental growth in the southwestern Altaids. Published: 2026-05-29T06:44:36+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.645 (must_read)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108644

Relevance: 0.645 (must_read)

The South Tianshan, stretching for ∼2500 km, marks the orogenic termination of the southwestern Altaids, with considerable continental growth and gold mineralization. However, despite extensive study, its tectonic nature and detailed evolutionary history remain controversial, and their link to continental growth and gold mineralization are insufficiently constrained. Here, we present a systematic study of the Late Carboniferous quartz diorite porphyrites, granodiorite porphyries and monzogranites from the Chore gold field and adjacent areas in the Tajik South Tianshan. LA–ICP–MS and LA–MC–ICP–MS zircon U–Pb dating of these intrusions yields magma crystallization ages of ∼308–300 Ma. The quartz diorite porphyrites and granodiorite porphyries are adakitic, with high Na 2 O (4.59–5.18 wt%), high Sr/Y (61–78) and relatively depleted isotopic compositions (e.g., ε Nd ( t ) = +0.1 to +1.3), indicating derivation from partial melting of the subducting Turkestan oceanic slab (MORB + minor overlying sediment). Minor pyrite occurs within the localized areas of the adakitic intrusions, which contains up to 1.76 ppm Au, indicating associated gold enrichment. The monzogranites are high-K calc-alkaline rocks that exhibit greater compositional variability. They possess enriched Sr–Nd–Hf isotopes and slightly high δ 66 Zn values (ca. +0.35‰), consistent with melting of a quartzofeldspathic crustal source followed by mixing with minor mantle-derived magmas. The discovery of slab-derived adakitic rocks supports the view that the South Tianshan consists mainly of accretionary complex. Based on available data, we suggest that nearly trench-orthogonal tearing of the subducting Turkestan oceanic lithosphere, accompanied by slab window formation, could have occurred at ∼308–300 Ma. This process induced partial melting of both slab and crustal materials, generated a southward younging magmatic trend, and drove gold enrichment in the region. Additionally, we propose that slab melting, mantle-derived magma intrusion and the accretion of mantle-derived rocks are important ways to continental growth in the southwestern Altaids.

item.title: Petrogenesis of Late Triassic high-Mg diorites and adakitic rocks in the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt and implications for Paleo-Tethyan evolution
item.dc:creator: Yu Zhao
Yongmei Zhang
Xuexiang Gu
Yunhang Hu
Jianhua Han
Wei Chen
Hao Shi
Huadong Ma
Bingyu Zhu
item.description: Gold mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt, western China, is widely regarded as a consequence of Paleo-Tethyan evolution, although the geodynamic processes responsible for magma generation and metal enrichment remain controversial. Here, we investigate the magmatic rocks exposed in the Qukulekedong Au Sb district of the western East Kunlun Orogenic Belt to constrain the Late Triassic tectono-magmatic evolution and its metallogenic significance. Zircon U Pb dating reveals two distinct Late Triassic magmatic episodes: emplacement of diorites at ca. 237 Ma, followed by the formation of high-Mg diorites and adakitic rocks at ca. 225–212 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that the early diorites were derived from partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subducted sediment-derived melts and subsequently evolved through extensive fractional crystallization. The late high-Mg diorites were generated by 10–20% partial melting of a garnet–spinel peridotite source in the lithospheric mantle that had been metasomatized by slab-derived fluids, triggered by asthenospheric upwelling, whereas the coeval adakitic rocks were produced by partial melting of compositionally heterogeneous thickened lower crust. Together with regional geological evidence, these results support a revised geodynamic model for the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt involving Paleo-Tethyan subduction prior to ca. 237 Ma, “soft” collision between the East Kunlun and Bayan Har terranes at 237–227 Ma, and slab break-off during post-collisional extension at 227–201 Ma. The slab break-off event likely promoted asthenospheric upwelling and widespread melting of metasomatized mantle, thereby generating fertile magmatism and creating favourable conditions for the development of the East Kunlun Au province. Published: 2026-05-29T15:50:56+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.634 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yu Zhao; Yongmei Zhang; Xuexiang Gu; Yunhang Hu; Jianhua Han; Wei Chen; Hao Shi; Huadong Ma; Bingyu Zhu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108649

Relevance: 0.634 (must_read)

Gold mineralization in the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt, western China, is widely regarded as a consequence of Paleo-Tethyan evolution, although the geodynamic processes responsible for magma generation and metal enrichment remain controversial. Here, we investigate the magmatic rocks exposed in the Qukulekedong Au Sb district of the western East Kunlun Orogenic Belt to constrain the Late Triassic tectono-magmatic evolution and its metallogenic significance. Zircon U Pb dating reveals two distinct Late Triassic magmatic episodes: emplacement of diorites at ca. 237 Ma, followed by the formation of high-Mg diorites and adakitic rocks at ca. 225–212 Ma. Geochemical and isotopic data indicate that the early diorites were derived from partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subducted sediment-derived melts and subsequently evolved through extensive fractional crystallization. The late high-Mg diorites were generated by 10–20% partial melting of a garnet–spinel peridotite source in the lithospheric mantle that had been metasomatized by slab-derived fluids, triggered by asthenospheric upwelling, whereas the coeval adakitic rocks were produced by partial melting of compositionally heterogeneous thickened lower crust. Together with regional geological evidence, these results support a revised geodynamic model for the East Kunlun Orogenic Belt involving Paleo-Tethyan subduction prior to ca. 237 Ma, “soft” collision between the East Kunlun and Bayan Har terranes at 237–227 Ma, and slab break-off during post-collisional extension at 227–201 Ma. The slab break-off event likely promoted asthenospheric upwelling and widespread melting of metasomatized mantle, thereby generating fertile magmatism and creating favourable conditions for the development of the East Kunlun Au province.

item.title: Late cretaceous large-scale melting of the Neo-Tethyan oceanic slab in southern Tibet
item.dc:creator: Cheng Li
Lin Ma
Andrew C. Kerr
Qiang Wang
Zhiwei Yu
Wei Qiao
item.description: The role of oceanic crustal melting in arc magmatism remains debated due to thermal constraints (> 800 °C) and limited observations. We present evidence from a newly identified 500 km-long adakite belt in the Neo-Tethyan subduction zone, southern Tibet, showing that adakites originated from melting of Neo-Tethyan oceanic crust, with minor sediment contributions, followed by mantle interaction and fractional crystallization. An E -W trending, high-temperature (> 800 °C), hydrous (H₂O > 3 wt%) magmatic zone, N-S geochemical-petrological zonation, and magma migration toward the suture, coeval with Late Cretaceous magmatic flare-up, are attributed to enhanced thermal gradients and slab dehydration during Neo-Tethyan rollback. Subduction decarbonation modulated magma composition via water activity, while differential melting of upper/lower oceanic crust influenced arc geochemistry and mantle heterogeneity. These findings bridge slab-scale dynamics with melt generation, offering a unified framework for understanding arc magma composition, continental evolution, and mantle heterogeneity. Published: 2026-05-27T15:50:07+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.630 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cheng Li; Lin Ma; Andrew C. Kerr; Qiang Wang; Zhiwei Yu; Wei Qiao

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108635

Relevance: 0.630 (must_read)

The role of oceanic crustal melting in arc magmatism remains debated due to thermal constraints (> 800 °C) and limited observations. We present evidence from a newly identified 500 km-long adakite belt in the Neo-Tethyan subduction zone, southern Tibet, showing that adakites originated from melting of Neo-Tethyan oceanic crust, with minor sediment contributions, followed by mantle interaction and fractional crystallization. An E -W trending, high-temperature (> 800 °C), hydrous (H₂O > 3 wt%) magmatic zone, N-S geochemical-petrological zonation, and magma migration toward the suture, coeval with Late Cretaceous magmatic flare-up, are attributed to enhanced thermal gradients and slab dehydration during Neo-Tethyan rollback. Subduction decarbonation modulated magma composition via water activity, while differential melting of upper/lower oceanic crust influenced arc geochemistry and mantle heterogeneity. These findings bridge slab-scale dynamics with melt generation, offering a unified framework for understanding arc magma composition, continental evolution, and mantle heterogeneity.

item.title: Combining zircon age, Hf-O isotopes and amphibole composition to assess the Cu-Ni-Co mineralization potential in East Tianshan mafic-ultramafic intrusions
item.dc:creator: Kezhang Qin
Dongmei Tang
Chao Li
Noreen J. Evans
Wenlong Xie
Junjie Yang
item.description: Zircon age and Hf-O isotopes reflect a depleted mantle source and low degree of contamination relate to barren intrusions; concentrated amphibole compositions reveal fractional crystallization of silicate melt and large-scale sulfide segregation. Together, they provide a powerful tool for identifying the mineralization potential of mafic-ultramafic rocks and may enhance exploration strategies at the southern margin of the CAOB. Zircon age and Hf-O isotopes reflect a depleted mantle source and low degree of contamination relate to barren intrusions; concentrated amphibole compositions reveal fractional crystallization of silicate melt and large-scale sulfide segregation. Together, they provide a powerful tool for identifying the mineralization potential of mafic-ultramafic rocks and may enhance exploration strategies at the southern margin of the CAOB.

item.title: Ages and geochemistry of Cretaceous igneous rocks record the transition from compression to extension in Fujian Province, South China
item.dc:creator: Yuelan Kang
Yuruo Shi
J. Lawford Anderson
Huiyi Sun
item.description: Late Mesozoic magmatism in South China provides critical insights into East Asia continental margin evolution. Cretaceous magmatic rocks in coastal Fujian Province, South China, record the coupled magmatic-tectonic evolution of this area, yet key questions remain regarding magma source transformation, timing of the compressional-extensional transition, and its driving mechanisms. This study focused on Cretaceous magmatic rocks in eastern Fujian, South China, employing zircon U-Pb dating, zircon Hf isotope analyses, and whole-rock analyses. These samples are categorized as undifferentiated I-type granites, highly differentiated I-type granites, and A-type granites. The I-type granites were derived from ancient crustal melting with crust-mantle interaction, while the A-type granites were generated by high-temperature and anhydrous remelting of enriched crustal components, with plagioclase fractionation in an extensional environment. In combination with available geochemical data in this area, magma sources shifted from 130 Ma to 80 Ma due to changing crustal contributions and enhanced fractional crystallization accompanied by melt extraction from the magma chamber. The following dynamic model is proposed: During 130−110 Ma, Paleo-Pacific plate subduction caused compression and thickened the crust, generating I-type granites via crust-mantle hybridization and crystallization differentiation. From 110 Ma to 100 Ma, slab rollback increased the subduction angle, triggering asthenospheric upwelling and lithospheric delamination, shifting stress from compression to extension. Lithospheric thinning dominated the 100−80 Ma interval, during which extensional tectonics facilitated the formation of A-type granites. Published: 2026-06-02T19:01:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.612 (consider)

Late Mesozoic magmatism in South China provides critical insights into East Asia continental margin evolution. Cretaceous magmatic rocks in coastal Fujian Province, South China, record the coupled magmatic-tectonic evolution of this area, yet key questions remain regarding magma source transformation, timing of the compressional-extensional transition, and its driving mechanisms. This study focused on Cretaceous magmatic rocks in eastern Fujian, South China, employing zircon U-Pb dating, zircon Hf isotope analyses, and whole-rock analyses. These samples are categorized as undifferentiated I-type granites, highly differentiated I-type granites, and A-type granites. The I-type granites were derived from ancient crustal melting with crust-mantle interaction, while the A-type granites were generated by high-temperature and anhydrous remelting of enriched crustal components, with plagioclase fractionation in an extensional environment. In combination with available geochemical data in this area, magma sources shifted from 130 Ma to 80 Ma due to changing crustal contributions and enhanced fractional crystallization accompanied by melt extraction from the magma chamber. The following dynamic model is proposed: During 130−110 Ma, Paleo-Pacific plate subduction caused compression and thickened the crust, generating I-type granites via crust-mantle hybridization and crystallization differentiation. From 110 Ma to 100 Ma, slab rollback increased the subduction angle, triggering asthenospheric upwelling and lithospheric delamination, shifting stress from compression to extension. Lithospheric thinning dominated the 100−80 Ma interval, during which extensional tectonics facilitated the formation of A-type granites.

item.title: Formation of Continental Intraplate Basalts with Coexistence of Alkaline and Tholeiitic Suites and their Geochemical Transition from Upwelling of Fractured Oceanic Slabs
item.dc:creator: Xun Yu
Zhifei Liu
Gang Zeng
Li-Hui Chen
Pham Nhu Sang
Long Van Hoang
Youqiang Yu
Jian Zhao
Julin Zhang
item.description: Abstract Intraplate basalts, exhibiting a continuous geochemical spectrum from alkaline to tholeiitic compositions, are extensively distributed along the continental margins of eastern and southeastern Eurasia, where subducted oceanic slabs are stagnant in the mantle transition zone (MTZ). The genetic relationship between the coexistence of alkaline and tholeiitic magmatism, the alkaline-tholeiitic transition, and upper mantle dynamics remains enigmatic. We present Sr, Nd, Mg, and Fe isotopic data, combined with major and trace elemental compositions, for the Late Cenozoic Buon Ma Thuot (BMT) and Pleiku (PLK) basalts from southern Vietnam to investigate these mantle processes. Changes in Mg and Fe isotopic values of these basalts are associated with their alkaline-tholeiitic transition. Besides of higher MgO, TiO2, CaO/Al2O3, and lower SiO2, alkaline basalts exhibit lower δ 26Mg (−0.32‰ – -0.43‰) and higher δ 57Fe values (+0.16‰ – +0.37‰) than tholeiitic basalts (i.e. δ 57Fe varies from + 0.09‰ to + 0.21‰) which resemble normal mid-ocean ridge basalts. Trace element ratios and Sr and Nd isotopes of the studied basalts vary spatially. BMT basalts display Ce/Pb (18.4–24.2) and Nb/Th (9.80–11.3) akin to those of oceanic crust, coupled with elevated positive Sr anomalies (Sr/Sr* = 1.10–1.56) and constant low 87Sr/86Sr ratios, indicating the presence of recycled lower oceanic crust in their mantle source. In contrast, mantle source of PLK basalts appears to incorporate more recycled sediments, as indicated by their lower Ce/Pb (13.0–23.1) and Nb/Th (7.16–9.43), along with elevated 87Sr/86Sr ratios and decreased εNd values. The alkaline-tholeiitic transition in both BMT and PLK basalts arises from differences in their source lithologies and melting processes. Spatial geochemical variations can be further ascribed to the small-scale lateral heterogeneity of the mantle, which arises from the recycling of various segments of subducted slabs. Given that BMT basalts erupted prior to PLK basalts, and considering the geophysical evidences (such as receiver function investigations and P-wave velocity models) indicating slab edges and mantle upwelling in the MTZ, we suggest that the observed geochemical variations in intraplate basalts are primarily driven by the upward flow of fractured oceanic slabs, facilitated by slab tearing processes occurring within the MTZ. Published: 2026-05-28T11:47:49+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.611 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xun Yu; Zhifei Liu; Gang Zeng; Li-Hui Chen; Pham Nhu Sang; Long Van Hoang; Youqiang Yu; Jian Zhao; Julin Zhang

Journal: Journal of Petrology

DOI: 10.1093/petrology/egag048

Relevance: 0.611 (consider)

Abstract Intraplate basalts, exhibiting a continuous geochemical spectrum from alkaline to tholeiitic compositions, are extensively distributed along the continental margins of eastern and southeastern Eurasia, where subducted oceanic slabs are stagnant in the mantle transition zone (MTZ). The genetic relationship between the coexistence of alkaline and tholeiitic magmatism, the alkaline-tholeiitic transition, and upper mantle dynamics remains enigmatic. We present Sr, Nd, Mg, and Fe isotopic data, combined with major and trace elemental compositions, for the Late Cenozoic Buon Ma Thuot (BMT) and Pleiku (PLK) basalts from southern Vietnam to investigate these mantle processes. Changes in Mg and Fe isotopic values of these basalts are associated with their alkaline-tholeiitic transition. Besides of higher MgO, TiO2, CaO/Al2O3, and lower SiO2, alkaline basalts exhibit lower δ 26Mg (−0.32‰ – -0.43‰) and higher δ 57Fe values (+0.16‰ – +0.37‰) than tholeiitic basalts (i.e. δ 57Fe varies from + 0.09‰ to + 0.21‰) which resemble normal mid-ocean ridge basalts. Trace element ratios and Sr and Nd isotopes of the studied basalts vary spatially. BMT basalts display Ce/Pb (18.4–24.2) and Nb/Th (9.80–11.3) akin to those of oceanic crust, coupled with elevated positive Sr anomalies (Sr/Sr* = 1.10–1.56) and constant low 87Sr/86Sr ratios, indicating the presence of recycled lower oceanic crust in their mantle source. In contrast, mantle source of PLK basalts appears to incorporate more recycled sediments, as indicated by their lower Ce/Pb (13.0–23.1) and Nb/Th (7.16–9.43), along with elevated 87Sr/86Sr ratios and decreased εNd values. The alkaline-tholeiitic transition in both BMT and PLK basalts arises from differences in their source lithologies and melting processes. Spatial geochemical variations can be further ascribed to the small-scale lateral heterogeneity of the mantle, which arises from the recycling of various segments of subducted slabs. Given that BMT basalts erupted prior to PLK basalts, and considering the geophysical evidences (such as receiver function investigations and P-wave velocity models) indicating slab edges and mantle upwelling in the MTZ, we suggest that the observed geochemical variations in intraplate basalts are primarily driven by the upward flow of fractured oceanic slabs, facilitated by slab tearing processes occurring within the MTZ.

item.title: Zircon Hf-O Isotopes and geochemistry of TTG in the basement of the Bohai Bay Basin: Petrogenesis and implications for ∼3.1 Ga crustal growth in the North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Tianyi Bian
Fanchao Meng
Andrew C. Kerr
Yonghua Liu
item.description: The Mesozoic-Cenozoic succession of the Bohai Bay Basin (BBB), located within the Eastern Block of the North China Craton (NCC), overlies a heterogeneous Archean basement. This sedimentary cover obscured insights into the region's early crustal evolution. To constrain the cryptic Archean history of the BBB, we present new in-situ zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Hf-O isotope data for TTG gneisses recovered from deep drill cores. This new data provides constraints on crustal growth and evolution of the Precambrian basement under the BBB in the NCC. Magmatic zircon U-Pb dating reveals two distinct episodes of crustal generation at ∼3.1 Ga and ∼2.5 Ga. The Mesoarchean (∼3.1 Ga) TTG rocks differ from previously published TTGs of the NCC. They exhibit high-K calc-alkaline affinities, with pronounced enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements (LILE) and depletion in high-field-strength elements (HFSE), consistent with generation from evolved granitoid magmas. Isotopic data further highlight their distinct character: highly positive εHf(t) values point to a juvenile mantle source, while their unusually low δ 18 O signatures indicate incorporation of isotopically light material during magma genesis. These isotopic signatures, specifically the δ 18 O-depletion, suggest that the petrogenesis of the ∼3.1 Ga TTGs involved the fluxing of fluids derived from hydrothermally altered oceanic slabs. Our results, integrated with previous studies, lead to three main conclusions. First, the Bohai Bay Basin experienced two major Archean magmatic events at ∼3.1 Ga and ∼ 2.5 Ga. Second, the ∼3.1 Ga TTG magmatism was influenced by oceanic fluids introduced during slab subduction. Third, the tectonic setting of the BBB region in the eastern NCC at ∼3.1 Ga reflects the combined effects of mantle plume activity and plate subduction. These findings provide important constraints on the tectonic evolution of the Eastern Block of the North China Craton. Published: 2026-05-29T06:45:04+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.601 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tianyi Bian; Fanchao Meng; Andrew C. Kerr; Yonghua Liu

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108643

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

The Mesozoic-Cenozoic succession of the Bohai Bay Basin (BBB), located within the Eastern Block of the North China Craton (NCC), overlies a heterogeneous Archean basement. This sedimentary cover obscured insights into the region's early crustal evolution. To constrain the cryptic Archean history of the BBB, we present new in-situ zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Hf-O isotope data for TTG gneisses recovered from deep drill cores. This new data provides constraints on crustal growth and evolution of the Precambrian basement under the BBB in the NCC. Magmatic zircon U-Pb dating reveals two distinct episodes of crustal generation at ∼3.1 Ga and ∼2.5 Ga. The Mesoarchean (∼3.1 Ga) TTG rocks differ from previously published TTGs of the NCC. They exhibit high-K calc-alkaline affinities, with pronounced enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements (LILE) and depletion in high-field-strength elements (HFSE), consistent with generation from evolved granitoid magmas. Isotopic data further highlight their distinct character: highly positive εHf(t) values point to a juvenile mantle source, while their unusually low δ 18 O signatures indicate incorporation of isotopically light material during magma genesis. These isotopic signatures, specifically the δ 18 O-depletion, suggest that the petrogenesis of the ∼3.1 Ga TTGs involved the fluxing of fluids derived from hydrothermally altered oceanic slabs. Our results, integrated with previous studies, lead to three main conclusions. First, the Bohai Bay Basin experienced two major Archean magmatic events at ∼3.1 Ga and ∼ 2.5 Ga. Second, the ∼3.1 Ga TTG magmatism was influenced by oceanic fluids introduced during slab subduction. Third, the tectonic setting of the BBB region in the eastern NCC at ∼3.1 Ga reflects the combined effects of mantle plume activity and plate subduction. These findings provide important constraints on the tectonic evolution of the Eastern Block of the North China Craton.

item.title: Crustal anatectic origin of the Tashan carbonatites in the southern margin of the North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Mao-Xue Chen
Zhi-Hui Zha
Mei-Li Yang
Wei Zhang
Wei Terry Chen
item.description: Most carbonatites worldwide were documented to originate from mantle-derived magmas. Carbonatites formed by the melting of sedimentary carbonate rocks were rarely reported, but the principal processes of formation, as well as economic significance, remain poorly constrained. In this paper, we investigated the newly discovered Tashan carbonatites in the southern margin of the North China Craton, Qinling orogenic belt. The Tashan carbonatites are present as dikes intruding the Neoproterozoic biotite syenite, associated with fenitization along the contacts. Monazite U Pb dating shows that the Tashan carbonatites were emplaced at ca. 401 Ma. These carbonatites have low REE concentrations ( total REE as 8.23 to 20.33 ppm) and exhibit unusual positive Ce anomalies in the chondrite-normalized REE patterns. They are enriched in large-ion lithophile elements (U, Sr, and Pb) and depleted in high-field strength elements (Nb, Ta, and Ti), and exhibit high δ 13 C V-PDB (−1.17 to 1.24‰) and δ 18 O V-SMOW (13.37 to 16.87‰) values. They have remarkably high ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr) i (0.71325–0.71935) and low ε Nd (t) (−8.26 to −6.08) values. These results show that these carbonatites are geochemically different from mantle-derived carbonatites but comparable to sedimentary carbonates. It is suggested that the Tashan carbonatites were formed by anatectic melting of buried dolomitic marble from the Taihua Group, i.e., basement of the southern margin of the North China Craton, during delamination and extension of Qinling orogenic belt in the Paleozoic. Our results highlight that the crustal carbonatites reflect metamorphic, magmatic, and tectonic events and may shed light on the tectonic evolution of orogenic process in specific regions. Published: 2026-05-29T15:50:46+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.600 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Mao-Xue Chen; Zhi-Hui Zha; Mei-Li Yang; Wei Zhang; Wei Terry Chen

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108650

Relevance: 0.600 (consider)

Most carbonatites worldwide were documented to originate from mantle-derived magmas. Carbonatites formed by the melting of sedimentary carbonate rocks were rarely reported, but the principal processes of formation, as well as economic significance, remain poorly constrained. In this paper, we investigated the newly discovered Tashan carbonatites in the southern margin of the North China Craton, Qinling orogenic belt. The Tashan carbonatites are present as dikes intruding the Neoproterozoic biotite syenite, associated with fenitization along the contacts. Monazite U Pb dating shows that the Tashan carbonatites were emplaced at ca. 401 Ma. These carbonatites have low REE concentrations ( total REE as 8.23 to 20.33 ppm) and exhibit unusual positive Ce anomalies in the chondrite-normalized REE patterns. They are enriched in large-ion lithophile elements (U, Sr, and Pb) and depleted in high-field strength elements (Nb, Ta, and Ti), and exhibit high δ 13 C V-PDB (−1.17 to 1.24‰) and δ 18 O V-SMOW (13.37 to 16.87‰) values. They have remarkably high ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr) i (0.71325–0.71935) and low ε Nd (t) (−8.26 to −6.08) values. These results show that these carbonatites are geochemically different from mantle-derived carbonatites but comparable to sedimentary carbonates. It is suggested that the Tashan carbonatites were formed by anatectic melting of buried dolomitic marble from the Taihua Group, i.e., basement of the southern margin of the North China Craton, during delamination and extension of Qinling orogenic belt in the Paleozoic. Our results highlight that the crustal carbonatites reflect metamorphic, magmatic, and tectonic events and may shed light on the tectonic evolution of orogenic process in specific regions.

item.title: Mesoarchean crustal growth in the Jiaobei Terrane, North China Craton: insights from plagioclase accumulation signatures in Laiyang TTG gneisses
item.dc:creator: Wen Zhang
Pinghua Liu
Lilin Du
Chonghui Yang
Guangyu Huang
Hua Xiang
Wanpeng Zhou
Jianlong Wang
Qianyu Li
Guochun Zhao
item.description: Distinguishing between deep subduction-related magmatism and intracrustal differentiation is critical for reconstructing the tectonic evolution of the North China Craton. In this study, we report zircon U–Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, Lu–Hf isotopes, and phase equilibrium modeling results for newly identified Mesoarchean TTG gneisses in the Laiyang area of the Jiaobei Terrane. Zircon U–Pb dating constrains two magmatic pulses at ca. 3.0 Ga (Xiabujia and Jiaojiadian trondhjemitic gneisses) and ca. 2.86 Ga (Beizhanggezhuang tonalitic gneisses). Isotopic and geochemical constraints indicate that these rocks originated from the partial melting of ca. 3.2 Ga juvenile mafic crust at low-to-medium pressures. Although the suite is predominantly of low-to-medium pressure affinity, sporadic samples exhibit high Sr/Y ratios and positive Eu anomalies resembling high-pressure TTGs. Phase equilibrium modeling indicates that these “high-pressure” signatures are not indicators of deep eclogite-facies melting, but are artifacts resulting from plagioclase accumulation at shallower depths. We prefer a predominantly vertical tectonic model involving the reworking of a thickened oceanic plateau, possibly facilitated by enhanced mantle heat input. Published: 2026-05-31T14:03:59+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.599 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wen Zhang; Pinghua Liu; Lilin Du; Chonghui Yang; Guangyu Huang; Hua Xiang; Wanpeng Zhou; Jianlong Wang; Qianyu Li; Guochun Zhao

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108159

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

Distinguishing between deep subduction-related magmatism and intracrustal differentiation is critical for reconstructing the tectonic evolution of the North China Craton. In this study, we report zircon U–Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, Lu–Hf isotopes, and phase equilibrium modeling results for newly identified Mesoarchean TTG gneisses in the Laiyang area of the Jiaobei Terrane. Zircon U–Pb dating constrains two magmatic pulses at ca. 3.0 Ga (Xiabujia and Jiaojiadian trondhjemitic gneisses) and ca. 2.86 Ga (Beizhanggezhuang tonalitic gneisses). Isotopic and geochemical constraints indicate that these rocks originated from the partial melting of ca. 3.2 Ga juvenile mafic crust at low-to-medium pressures. Although the suite is predominantly of low-to-medium pressure affinity, sporadic samples exhibit high Sr/Y ratios and positive Eu anomalies resembling high-pressure TTGs. Phase equilibrium modeling indicates that these “high-pressure” signatures are not indicators of deep eclogite-facies melting, but are artifacts resulting from plagioclase accumulation at shallower depths. We prefer a predominantly vertical tectonic model involving the reworking of a thickened oceanic plateau, possibly facilitated by enhanced mantle heat input.

item.title: Global Variation of Mid-Ocean Ridge Basalt Chemistry: Conditions and Composition of the Source Mantle and Physical Properties of the Ridges
item.dc:creator: JUN-ICHI KIMURA
JAMES B GILL
KAJ HOERNLE
SHIGEAKI ONO
item.description: Abstract We investigate relationships between the chemistry of global Mid-Ocean Ridge Basalts (MORBs), the conditions and composition of their source mantle, and the physical properties of the ridges. To accomplish this, major and trace element compositions of primary MORB are estimated by a back-calculation code PRIMACALC3 assisted by a thermodynamically constrained forward fractional crystallisation model COMAGMAT 3.72 with an additional PRIMELT3-P model to ensure mantle equilibrium of the primary melts. We then apply Ocean Basalt Simulator version 1 (OBS1), a thermodynamically constrained forward calculation code for adiabatic melting of a pyroxenite-bearing mantle peridotite, to the trace element compositions of our primary MORBs. Melting conditions and compositions of the MORB source mantle, such as pressure (P), degree of partial melting (F), mantle potential temperature (Tp), fractions of pyroxenite (fPY) and ambient depleted mantle (fDM), and the degree of depletion of the final residual mantle (FPD) are estimated by the two models. The results show that (a) MORBs are derived from mantle with Tp = 1250 − 1520 °C at F = 3 − 31 %, and both are high beneath shallow ridges; (b) pyroxenites in the source mantle peridotite are both recycled MORBs and ocean island basalts (OIBs) in origin and their fractions are estimated to be 0 − 6 % with a high fraction in deep and cold ridges; (c) the fraction of OIB-type pyroxenite is highest at shallow and hot ridges; (d) the degree of depletion of the residual peridotite is highest at shallow and hot ridges, and (e) the isotopic compositions of the OIB-type pyroxenites have both EM1 and HIMU signatures, while that of the MORB-type pyroxenites and the ambient mantle peridotites have depleted compositions. The high-Tp mantle containing recycled OIB-type pyroxenite has a deep plume origin. It forms ridges shallower than 2500 m. b. s. l. due to thermal and chemical buoyancy relative to the ambient upper mantle. In contrast, deeper ridges have a narrower and lower Tp range, and a lesser but variable amount of peridotite depletion. The depth of mid-ocean ridges is controlled by both Tp and relative mantle fertility. Published: 2026-05-27T11:43:49+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.597 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: JUN-ICHI KIMURA; JAMES B GILL; KAJ HOERNLE; SHIGEAKI ONO

Journal: Journal of Petrology

DOI: 10.1093/petrology/egag047

Relevance: 0.597 (consider)

Abstract We investigate relationships between the chemistry of global Mid-Ocean Ridge Basalts (MORBs), the conditions and composition of their source mantle, and the physical properties of the ridges. To accomplish this, major and trace element compositions of primary MORB are estimated by a back-calculation code PRIMACALC3 assisted by a thermodynamically constrained forward fractional crystallisation model COMAGMAT 3.72 with an additional PRIMELT3-P model to ensure mantle equilibrium of the primary melts. We then apply Ocean Basalt Simulator version 1 (OBS1), a thermodynamically constrained forward calculation code for adiabatic melting of a pyroxenite-bearing mantle peridotite, to the trace element compositions of our primary MORBs. Melting conditions and compositions of the MORB source mantle, such as pressure (P), degree of partial melting (F), mantle potential temperature (Tp), fractions of pyroxenite (fPY) and ambient depleted mantle (fDM), and the degree of depletion of the final residual mantle (FPD) are estimated by the two models. The results show that (a) MORBs are derived from mantle with Tp = 1250 − 1520 °C at F = 3 − 31 %, and both are high beneath shallow ridges; (b) pyroxenites in the source mantle peridotite are both recycled MORBs and ocean island basalts (OIBs) in origin and their fractions are estimated to be 0 − 6 % with a high fraction in deep and cold ridges; (c) the fraction of OIB-type pyroxenite is highest at shallow and hot ridges; (d) the degree of depletion of the residual peridotite is highest at shallow and hot ridges, and (e) the isotopic compositions of the OIB-type pyroxenites have both EM1 and HIMU signatures, while that of the MORB-type pyroxenites and the ambient mantle peridotites have depleted compositions. The high-Tp mantle containing recycled OIB-type pyroxenite has a deep plume origin. It forms ridges shallower than 2500 m. b. s. l. due to thermal and chemical buoyancy relative to the ambient upper mantle. In contrast, deeper ridges have a narrower and lower Tp range, and a lesser but variable amount of peridotite depletion. The depth of mid-ocean ridges is controlled by both Tp and relative mantle fertility.

item.title: Temporal and spatial trends in mantle metasomatism and redox state beneath the Kimberley Craton (Northwest Australia)
item.dc:creator: Z.J. Sudholz
A.L. Jaques
G.M. Yaxley
D.C. Hezel
C. Frigo
item.description: The Kimberley Craton of northwest Australia hosts a suite of kimberlites, lamproites and ultramafic lamprophyres that range in age from ~1257 (Argyle) to ~20 Ma (Ellendale). Mantle xenocrysts and xenoliths entrained in these rocks provide the opportunity to examine how the composition and structure of the cratonic lithospheric mantle has evolved through time. In this study we present the first oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) profile through the cratonic lithospheric mantle beneath the Ellendale and Argyle lamproite pipes. The f O 2 estimates are based on the atomic Fe 3+ / Σ " role="presentation"> Σ Σ Fe in garnet xenocrysts determined by the flank method accompanied by geothermobarometric and trace element data. The f O 2 values indicate a reduced lithosphere beneath the Argyle and Ellendale pipes, within the range of values found for other diamondiferous cratonic lithosphere, but with a systematic trend to more oxidized conditions (by up to 5 log units in f O 2 ) toward the base of the lithosphere. This trend is accompanied by increases in TiO 2 , FeOT, LREE (light rare-earth elements) and in V/Sc, Sm/Er and Zr/Y values with increasing temperature (T). Garnet xenocrysts with higher Fe 3+ / Σ " role="presentation"> Σ Σ Fe from the base of the lithosphere have sinusoidal REE patterns in contrast to the mostly LREE-depleted patterns of garnets at shallower levels in the lithosphere. The oxidation and enrichment of the deeper lithosphere is the result of metasomatism by infiltrating hydrous, possibly carbonate-bearing, silicate melts. At Argyle, the base of the lithosphere was also infiltrated by hydrous fluids that formed abundant Proterozoic eclogitic diamonds with subduction signatures. The composition, thickness and redox state of the lithosphere beneath the Argyle and Ellendale lamproites is similar apart from the base of the Argyle lithosphere being more oxidized and minor differences in the exact values for some trace element ratios (i.e., Ti/Eu, Zr/Y, and Sm/Yb). Existing clinopyroxene paleogeotherms indicate cool conductive paleogeothermal gradients with lithospheric thicknesses similar to those of seismic tomographic models. These results suggest that the Kimberley Craton has remained relatively stable since at least the Mesoproterozoic, despite the emplacement of lamproites, kimberlites and ultramafic lamprophyres at ~840–800 Ma and eruption of continental flood basalts of the Kalkarindji large igneous province (LIP) at 511 Ma. The long-term stability of the lithosphere of the Kimberley Craton has allowed the survival of diamonds of differing ages and the preservation of ancient, enriched mantle domains that gave rise to the Miocene lamproites with extremely enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopes that formed by prolonged unperturbed radiogenic ingrowth since the Paleoproterozoic. Published: 2026-05-29T06:44:51+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.596 (consider)

Relevance: 0.596 (consider)

The Kimberley Craton of northwest Australia hosts a suite of kimberlites, lamproites and ultramafic lamprophyres that range in age from ~1257 (Argyle) to ~20 Ma (Ellendale). Mantle xenocrysts and xenoliths entrained in these rocks provide the opportunity to examine how the composition and structure of the cratonic lithospheric mantle has evolved through time. In this study we present the first oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) profile through the cratonic lithospheric mantle beneath the Ellendale and Argyle lamproite pipes. The f O 2 estimates are based on the atomic Fe 3+ / Σ " role="presentation"> Σ Σ Fe in garnet xenocrysts determined by the flank method accompanied by geothermobarometric and trace element data. The f O 2 values indicate a reduced lithosphere beneath the Argyle and Ellendale pipes, within the range of values found for other diamondiferous cratonic lithosphere, but with a systematic trend to more oxidized conditions (by up to 5 log units in f O 2 ) toward the base of the lithosphere. This trend is accompanied by increases in TiO 2 , FeOT, LREE (light rare-earth elements) and in V/Sc, Sm/Er and Zr/Y values with increasing temperature (T). Garnet xenocrysts with higher Fe 3+ / Σ " role="presentation"> Σ Σ Fe from the base of the lithosphere have sinusoidal REE patterns in contrast to the mostly LREE-depleted patterns of garnets at shallower levels in the lithosphere. The oxidation and enrichment of the deeper lithosphere is the result of metasomatism by infiltrating hydrous, possibly carbonate-bearing, silicate melts. At Argyle, the base of the lithosphere was also infiltrated by hydrous fluids that formed abundant Proterozoic eclogitic diamonds with subduction signatures. The composition, thickness and redox state of the lithosphere beneath the Argyle and Ellendale lamproites is similar apart from the base of the Argyle lithosphere being more oxidized and minor differences in the exact values for some trace element ratios (i.e., Ti/Eu, Zr/Y, and Sm/Yb). Existing clinopyroxene paleogeotherms indicate cool conductive paleogeothermal gradients with lithospheric thicknesses similar to those of seismic tomographic models. These results suggest that the Kimberley Craton has remained relatively stable since at least the Mesoproterozoic, despite the emplacement of lamproites, kimberlites and ultramafic lamprophyres at ~840–800 Ma and eruption of continental flood basalts of the Kalkarindji large igneous province (LIP) at 511 Ma. The long-term stability of the lithosphere of the Kimberley Craton has allowed the survival of diamonds of differing ages and the preservation of ancient, enriched mantle domains that gave rise to the Miocene lamproites with extremely enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopes that formed by prolonged unperturbed radiogenic ingrowth since the Paleoproterozoic.

item.title: Early Carboniferous ophiolite discovery in central Mongolia: Expanding the record of subduction initiation in the Mongol-Okhotsk Ocean
item.dc:creator: Mingshuai Zhu
Daniel Pastor-Galán
Chongrui Tang
Shun Li
Dorjgochoo Sanchir
Laicheng Miao
Fuqin Zhang
Ariuntsetseg Ganbat
Munkhtsengel Baatar
Chimedtseren Anaad
Jilei Li
item.description: The subduction initiation dynamics and southwestern extension of the Mongol-Okhotsk suture zone in Central Asia have been enigmatic due to a scarcity of robust ophiolitic records. This study presents an integrated analysis of petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology conducted on the newly discovered Erdendalai ophiolite in the Dundgobi Province of Mongolia. The ophiolite mainly comprises serpentinized peridotite, gabbro, anorthosite, and chert. Zircon U-Pb dating of an anorthosite sample yields a crystallization age of 350 ± 4 Ma (early Carboniferous), which identifies this ophiolite as the oldest documented ophiolite within the Mongol-Okhotsk Ocean realm. Geochemically, the gabbroic rocks exhibit normal mid-ocean-ridge basalt−like signatures with slight depletion in high field strength elements and enrichment in large ion lithophile elements, resembling forearc basalts. The anorthosites are interpreted to have crystallized from hydrous, arc-related tholeiitic magmas derived from boninitic precursors. These features collectively indicate that the Erdendalai ophiolite formed in a supra-subduction zone setting, specifically an intra-oceanic forearc, during the early Carboniferous. Detrital zircon age spectra from the enclosing Khoidgobi Formation schists show a youngest peak at ca. 302 Ma, constraining the accretion and emplacement of the ophiolite to the latest Carboniferous or later. Our findings not only confirm the southwestern extension of the Mongol-Okhotsk suture but also provide critical evidence for the early Carboniferous subduction initiation of the Mongol-Okhotsk Ocean, likely facilitated by the presence of a continental ribbon rifted during the ocean’s opening. The distribution of supra-subduction zone ophiolites with different ages along the Mongol-Okhotsk suture zone may document lateral propagation of subduction initiation. Published: 2026-05-27T19:11:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.593 (consider)

The subduction initiation dynamics and southwestern extension of the Mongol-Okhotsk suture zone in Central Asia have been enigmatic due to a scarcity of robust ophiolitic records. This study presents an integrated analysis of petrography, geochemistry, and geochronology conducted on the newly discovered Erdendalai ophiolite in the Dundgobi Province of Mongolia. The ophiolite mainly comprises serpentinized peridotite, gabbro, anorthosite, and chert. Zircon U-Pb dating of an anorthosite sample yields a crystallization age of 350 ± 4 Ma (early Carboniferous), which identifies this ophiolite as the oldest documented ophiolite within the Mongol-Okhotsk Ocean realm. Geochemically, the gabbroic rocks exhibit normal mid-ocean-ridge basalt−like signatures with slight depletion in high field strength elements and enrichment in large ion lithophile elements, resembling forearc basalts. The anorthosites are interpreted to have crystallized from hydrous, arc-related tholeiitic magmas derived from boninitic precursors. These features collectively indicate that the Erdendalai ophiolite formed in a supra-subduction zone setting, specifically an intra-oceanic forearc, during the early Carboniferous. Detrital zircon age spectra from the enclosing Khoidgobi Formation schists show a youngest peak at ca. 302 Ma, constraining the accretion and emplacement of the ophiolite to the latest Carboniferous or later. Our findings not only confirm the southwestern extension of the Mongol-Okhotsk suture but also provide critical evidence for the early Carboniferous subduction initiation of the Mongol-Okhotsk Ocean, likely facilitated by the presence of a continental ribbon rifted during the ocean’s opening. The distribution of supra-subduction zone ophiolites with different ages along the Mongol-Okhotsk suture zone may document lateral propagation of subduction initiation.

item.title: New determination of deep mantle sulfur isotopic composition from Galápagos submarine glasses
item.dc:creator: I. Genot
P. Cartigny
M.D. Kurz
item.description: Whether high 3 He/ 4 He ratios in some ocean island basalts (OIB) reflect the remnant of an undegassed reservoir or the core interaction with the deep mantle is still debated. In this study, we bring additional constraints on the S origin in the high 3 He/ 4 He reservoir. We investigated submarine basaltic glasses from Fernandina volcano, Galápagos Archipelago, and from the Galápagos Spreading Center (GSC) to estimate the S-isotopic composition of the primitive mantle (high 3 He/ 4 He reservoir). Published: 2026-05-30T07:02:09+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.592 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: I. Genot; P. Cartigny; M.D. Kurz

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120125

Relevance: 0.592 (consider)

Whether high 3 He/ 4 He ratios in some ocean island basalts (OIB) reflect the remnant of an undegassed reservoir or the core interaction with the deep mantle is still debated. In this study, we bring additional constraints on the S origin in the high 3 He/ 4 He reservoir. We investigated submarine basaltic glasses from Fernandina volcano, Galápagos Archipelago, and from the Galápagos Spreading Center (GSC) to estimate the S-isotopic composition of the primitive mantle (high 3 He/ 4 He reservoir).

item.title: Origin and lithium enrichment mechanism of the largest individual Murong pegmatite dyke in Asia: Revealed by phosphate mineral chemistry and SrNd isotopes
item.dc:creator: Dabin Yue
Shilei Qiao
Xiaodong Chen
Fengli Liu
Bin Chen
item.description: The Murong pegmatite dyke hosting over 0.96 Mt. Li 2 O (average grade: 1.63%) in the eastern Songpan-Garze Fold Belt (SW China) is by far the largest individual pegmatite dyke in Asia. However, the origin of pegmatite and associated Li mineralization remain unclear. We report in this study the Mn-columbite U Pb dating, in-situ phosphate (triphylite, fairfieldite and apatite) mineral chemistry, apatite Sr and monazite Nd isotopic data to address these issues. The dating results indicate that the Murong pegmatite has two pegmatite-forming pulses of 201–197 Ma, and 192.7 Ma, respectively. The Murong pegmatite shows I Sr ratios (0.7362–0.7551) significantly higher and initial ε Nd values (ε Nd (t) = −10.4 to −7.6) lower, than the local contemporaneous granitic plutons and the Xikang Group metasediments as well. These data do not favor the traditional hypothesis that attributes the formation of pegmatite to the extreme fractional crystallization of parent granitic systems. Rather, the hypothesis of direct melting of metasediments is inferred. We suggest the Murong pegmatite was derived from melting of the late Proterozoic basement rocks in view of their consistent Sr Nd isotopes, and the source may be dominated by claystone-rich rocks formed by hydrothermal alteration of felsic volcanic tuffs based on the typically radiogenic Sr Nd isotopic features. Importantly, we recognized two stages of fluid-mineral interactions in the Murong pegmatite: an earlier replacement of Li-rich anhydrous triphylite by hydrous fairfieldite due to concentration of aqueous fluid, releasing abundant Li into fluid, and a later replacement of apatite 1 by a relatively Li-rich apatite 2. These findings demonstrate that (1) the Murong pegmatite-forming magma is highly enriched in Li even in the very beginning of magma evolution, thus suggesting that the pegmatite may originate from partial melting of a Li-rich claystone-dominated source, and (2) the dissolution of early crystallized Li-rich triphylite is crucial to the continued Li concentration in fluid phase, and thus to the Li mineralization. Published: 2026-05-29T15:15:10+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.587 (consider)

The Murong pegmatite dyke hosting over 0.96 Mt. Li 2 O (average grade: 1.63%) in the eastern Songpan-Garze Fold Belt (SW China) is by far the largest individual pegmatite dyke in Asia. However, the origin of pegmatite and associated Li mineralization remain unclear. We report in this study the Mn-columbite U Pb dating, in-situ phosphate (triphylite, fairfieldite and apatite) mineral chemistry, apatite Sr and monazite Nd isotopic data to address these issues. item.title: Anomalous Cu enrichment at the 49.28°E hydrothermal field on the ultraslow-spreading Southwest Indian Ridge: evidence from sulfide mineralogy, geochemistry and sulfur isotopes
item.dc:creator: Hang Ao
Shili Liao
Chunhui Tao
Wei Li
Kaiying Chen
Xianming Deng
Yuan Tian
Weifang Yang
Jin Liang
item.description: Copper content in sulfides on mid-ocean ridges (MOR) in controlled by the variation and abundance of Cu-rich minerals, that sulfides in ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal fields commonly contain higher Cu, compared with those in mafic-hosted hydrothermal fields. Recently, a new ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal field at 49.28°E was discovered on the ultraslow-spreading Southwest Indian Ridge (SWIR). Sulfides collected show remarkably high Cu contents (20.5–39.7 wt%, average 31.8 wt%), greater than those of typical ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal fields (0.07–33.98 wt%, average 17.94 wt%). In this study, we integrate sulfide mineralogy, bulk geochemistry and sulfur-isotope analysis of typical sulfide samples. Our findings indicate that the Cu-rich sulfides consist predominantly of primary chalcopyrite, bornite and secondary copper minerals, with bulk compositions enriched in Cu (∼26.88 ± 12.47 wt%, N = 19, 1SD), Au (∼5.1 ± 5.66 ppm), Co (∼730.96 ± 434.27 ppm), and Se (∼454.4 ± 247.21 ppm), while being depleted in Ni (∼7.46 ± 17.54 ppm). Additionally, these sulfides display relatively positive but uniform δ 34 S values, ranging from + 4.49 to + 6.9‰ (averaged + 5.49‰; N = 42), except for one value of + 2.3‰. These characteristics combined indicate deep ultramafic sourced metals, with restricted seawater entrainment (19–30%), and sulfide deposition occurring in a high-temperature (>300 °C), reducing, and relatively closed system. We propose that these conditions result from the immature Yuhuang detachment, which promotes early serpentinization of ultramafic rocks and low permeability, distinguishing from matured detachment controlled hydrothermal systems. Our results constrain hydrothermal formation at the end of immature detachment faults and provide new insights into ore-forming processes in ultramafic-hosted systems along ultraslow-spreading ridges. Published: 2026-06-01T07:22:22+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.584 (consider)

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

Relevance: 0.584 (consider)

Copper content in sulfides on mid-ocean ridges (MOR) in controlled by the variation and abundance of Cu-rich minerals, that sulfides in ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal fields commonly contain higher Cu, compared with those in mafic-hosted hydrothermal fields. Recently, a new ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal field at 49.28°E was discovered on the ultraslow-spreading Southwest Indian Ridge (SWIR). Sulfides collected show remarkably high Cu contents (20.5–39.7 wt%, average 31.8 wt%), greater than those of typical ultramafic-hosted hydrothermal fields (0.07–33.98 wt%, average 17.94 wt%). Our findings indicate that the Cu-rich sulfides consist predominantly of primary chalcopyrite, bornite and secondary copper minerals, with bulk compositions enriched in Cu (∼26.88 ± 12.47 wt%, N = 19, 1SD), Au (∼5.1 ± 5.66 ppm), Co (∼730.96 ± 434.27 ppm), and Se (∼454.4 ± 247.21 ppm), while being depleted in Ni (∼7.46 ± 17.54 ppm). Additionally, these sulfides display relatively positive but uniform δ 34 S values, ranging from + 4.49 to + 6.9‰ (averaged + 5.49‰; N = 42), except for one value of + 2.3‰. These characteristics combined indicate deep ultramafic sourced metals, with restricted seawater entrainment (19–30%), and sulfide deposition occurring in a high-temperature (>300 °C), reducing, and relatively closed system. We propose that these conditions result from the immature Yuhuang detachment, which promotes early serpentinization of ultramafic rocks and low permeability, distinguishing from matured detachment controlled hydrothermal systems. Our results constrain hydrothermal formation at the end of immature detachment faults and provide new insights into ore-forming processes in ultramafic-hosted systems along ultraslow-spreading ridges.

item.title: Independent evolution of Gondwana terranes along its northern margin during early Tethyan initiation
item.dc:creator: Mingjun Zheng
Jun Deng
Yongjun Lu
Huanchao Xu
Jiale Wang
Yingjie Wang
Xianyi Huang
Ke Miao
Xiang Sun
item.description: The late Ediacaran−early Paleozoic (580−420 Ma) represents a critical interval marked by the final Gondwana assembly and initiation of the Tethyan realm. The Lhasa terrane (southwestern China) is commonly regarded as part of the Indo-Australian margin tectonic system, yet its paleogeographic affinity and tectono-magmatic evolution remain debated. Here we present a systematic comparison of geological records from the Lhasa terrane with those from the South Qiangtang, Himalaya, and Sibumasu terranes as well as the Iran-Anatolia region. Our results reveal significant differences in tectono-magmatic evolution among terranes along the northern margin of Gondwana. The Lhasa terrane records a prolonged subduction-related history from 580 to 480 Ma, comparable to that of the Iran-Anatolia region, including low-angle subduction at 570−525 Ma, slab rollback at 525−510 Ma, and terrane accretion accompanied by slab break-off at 510−480 Ma. In contrast, terranes along the Indo-Australian margin record a coherent two-stage evolution, with 550−520 Ma ocean-island basalt−like magmatism related to the postcollisional extension stage of the Kuunga-Pinjarra orogen, followed by 500−420 Ma arc-backarc magmatism linked to Proto-Tethyan subduction. These contrasts demonstrate that the Lhasa terrane was not part of the Indo-Australian margin but evolved independently along the African margin of Gondwana, an interpretation further supported by metamorphic and detrital-zircon evidence. Our results refine the geodynamic framework of early Tethyan evolution, provide new constraints on the paleogeographic reconstruction of East Asian blocks, and offer insight into the spatial heterogeneity of Phanerozoic tin mineralization. Published: 2026-06-02T10:53:22+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.583 (consider)

Authors: Mingjun Zheng; Jun Deng; Yongjun Lu; Huanchao Xu; Jiale Wang; Yingjie Wang; Xianyi Huang; Ke Miao; Xiang Sun

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54872.1

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

The late Ediacaran−early Paleozoic (580−420 Ma) represents a critical interval marked by the final Gondwana assembly and initiation of the Tethyan realm. The Lhasa terrane (southwestern China) is commonly regarded as part of the Indo-Australian margin tectonic system, yet its paleogeographic affinity and tectono-magmatic evolution remain debated. Here we present a systematic comparison of geological records from the Lhasa terrane with those from the South Qiangtang, Himalaya, and Sibumasu terranes as well as the Iran-Anatolia region. Our results reveal significant differences in tectono-magmatic evolution among terranes along the northern margin of Gondwana. The Lhasa terrane records a prolonged subduction-related history from 580 to 480 Ma, comparable to that of the Iran-Anatolia region, including low-angle subduction at 570−525 Ma, slab rollback at 525−510 Ma, and terrane accretion accompanied by slab break-off at 510−480 Ma. In contrast, terranes along the Indo-Australian margin record a coherent two-stage evolution, with 550−520 Ma ocean-island basalt−like magmatism related to the postcollisional extension stage of the Kuunga-Pinjarra orogen, followed by 500−420 Ma arc-backarc magmatism linked to Proto-Tethyan subduction. These contrasts demonstrate that the Lhasa terrane was not part of the Indo-Australian margin but evolved independently along the African margin of Gondwana, an interpretation further supported by metamorphic and detrital-zircon evidence. Our results refine the geodynamic framework of early Tethyan evolution, provide new constraints on the paleogeographic reconstruction of East Asian blocks, and offer insight into the spatial heterogeneity of Phanerozoic tin mineralization.

item.title: Proterozoic crustal evolution and tectonic affinity of the Beidahe microcontinent in the North Qilian Orogenic Belt: Constraints from zircon U–Pb ages and Lu–Hf isotopes
item.dc:creator: Chao Shi
Chao Wang
Yonghe Wang
Xiaokui Sun
Shuai Zhang
Joseph G. Meert
Ke Liu
Houqi Wang
item.description: The North Qilian Orogenic Belt (NQOB) preserves key fragments of Precambrian continental crust along the northern margin of the Proto–Tethyan system. One such fragment is the Beidahe Block (BDHB) that forms the focus of this study. To resolve the crustal architecture, provenance, and tectonic affinity of the BDHB, we present zircon U–Pb and Lu–Hf data from Paleoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic metasedimentary and magmatic rocks in the western NQOB. Detrital zircon spectra from the Beidahe and Zhulongguan Groups yield maximum depositional ages of ca. 1.4 Ga and 728 Ma, respectively, and display major clusters at 2.45 Ga, 1.7–1.6 Ga, 1.55–1.30 Ga, and 0.95–0.74 Ga, indicating mixed sediment input from Archean–Paleoproterozoic basement and Mesoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic magmatic sources. Magmatic zircon ages of ca. 1.65 Ga (granitic gneiss), 1.09 Ga (garnet amphibolite), and a metamorphic age of 497 Ma record late Paleoproterozoic crustal growth, previously unrecognized Mesoproterozoic mafic magmatism, and Early Paleozoic high-grade metamorphism related to subduction initiation. Zircon Lu–Hf compositions are dominated by negative εHf(t) values with T DM2 model ages of 3.5–1.4 Ga, demonstrating extensive reworking of ancient continental crust with limited juvenile input during Mesoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic magmatism and sedimentation. Comparative detrital zircon analysis shows that the BDHB closely resembles the Central Qilian Block (CQB), sharing the characteristic ∼ 0.95 Ga and ∼ 1.60 Ga peaks while lacking the ∼ 1.45 Ga and ∼ 1.90 Ga signals typical of the Alxa Block. This affinity suggests that the BDHB formed a Rodinia-derived microcontinent along the southern margin of the North Qilian Ocean. The provenance pattern and metamorphic record collectively indicate a southward/bidirectional subduction polarity during Early Paleozoic Ocean closure. Published: 2026-05-30T04:49:59+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.582 (consider)

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

The North Qilian Orogenic Belt (NQOB) preserves key fragments of Precambrian continental crust along the northern margin of the Proto–Tethyan system. One such fragment is the Beidahe Block (BDHB) that forms the focus of this study. To resolve the crustal architecture, provenance, and tectonic affinity of the BDHB, we present zircon U–Pb and Lu–Hf data from Paleoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic metasedimentary and magmatic rocks in the western NQOB. Detrital zircon spectra from the Beidahe and Zhulongguan Groups yield maximum depositional ages of ca. 1.4 Ga and 728 Ma, respectively, and display major clusters at 2.45 Ga, 1.7–1.6 Ga, 1.55–1.30 Ga, and 0.95–0.74 Ga, indicating mixed sediment input from Archean–Paleoproterozoic basement and Mesoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic magmatic sources. Magmatic zircon ages of ca. 1.65 Ga (granitic gneiss), 1.09 Ga (garnet amphibolite), and a metamorphic age of 497 Ma record late Paleoproterozoic crustal growth, previously unrecognized Mesoproterozoic mafic magmatism, and Early Paleozoic high-grade metamorphism related to subduction initiation. Zircon Lu–Hf compositions are dominated by negative εHf(t) values with T DM2 model ages of 3.5–1.4 Ga, demonstrating extensive reworking of ancient continental crust with limited juvenile input during Mesoproterozoic–Neoproterozoic magmatism and sedimentation. Comparative detrital zircon analysis shows that the BDHB closely resembles the Central Qilian Block (CQB), sharing the characteristic ∼ 0.95 Ga and ∼ 1.60 Ga peaks while lacking the ∼ 1.45 Ga and ∼ 1.90 Ga signals typical of the Alxa Block. This affinity suggests that the BDHB formed a Rodinia-derived microcontinent along the southern margin of the North Qilian Ocean. The provenance pattern and metamorphic record collectively indicate a southward/bidirectional subduction polarity during Early Paleozoic Ocean closure.

item.title: Biotite unravels F-driven Mo mineralization and magmatic-hydrothermal evolution in the Chaijiagou porphyry deposit, North China
item.dc:creator: Fan Yang
Xiaoyu Ge
Zhenyu Qin
Lifeng Xu
Luyang Wang
Likun Yang
Hui Su
item.description: Porphyry molybdenum (Mo) deposits are the world's primary source of Mo and form through multi-stage processes involving source melting, magmatic differentiation, and fluid exsolution that drives hydrothermal Mo mineralization. The transition from magmatic to hydrothermal conditions is a critical phase in these magmatic-hydrothermal systems, yet its primary features are often obscured by subsequent overprinting, hindering direct observation. Biotite preserves a continuous record from magmatic crystallization to hydrothermal alteration, providing critical insights into this evolution. To quantitatively reconstruct the magmatic-hydrothermal transition and elucidate Mo enrichment mechanisms, this study utilizes in-situ geochemical analyses of various biotite generations from the Chaijiagou porphyry deposit, northern North China Block (NCB). Based on quantitative geochemical criteria, three biotite types were identified: early-stage magmatic biotite (Bt1), late-stage magmatic biotite (Bt2), and hydrothermal biotite (Bt3). Magmatic biotite shows a pseudo-A-type geochemical signature, indicating an extensional geodynamic setting related to lithospheric thinning of the NCB. Magmatic biotite crystallized at an average depth of 4.7 ± 0.7 km and temperature of ~705 ± 5 °C, under relatively oxidized conditions (ΔFMQ = +0.13 to +0.31, average 0.25 ± 0.03), which limited early sulfide saturation and promoted initial Mo enrichment. Progressive melt evolution through fractional crystallization is evidenced by systematic decreases in K/Rb ratios from Bt1 (76.8–281.1) to Bt2 (63.8–116.9), accompanied by enrichment in incompatible elements. Low halogen intercept values in magmatic biotite indicate an initially F-rich magmatic system that enhanced melt fractionation and metal fertility. During the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution, hydrothermal biotite (Bt3) records a shift toward high F fugacity, with IV(F) increasing to 1.66–1.74, IV(F/Cl) rising to 6.24–6.44, and IV(Cl) decreasing to −4.71 to −4.56. Meanwhile, log(f H2O /f HF ) ratios dropped from 2.02 to 2.04 in the magmatic stage to approximately 1.47 ± 0.03 in the hydrothermal stage. This elevated F activity significantly enhanced the solubility and transport capacity of Mo complexes. Large-scale molybdenite precipitation was triggered by cooling to ~400 ± 3 °C, coupled with previously documented fluid boiling and meteoric water influx, which destabilized these metal-transporting complexes. Collectively, these results highlight the potential of biotite geochemical signatures as a robust proxy for tracing the full magmatic evolution of highly fractionated porphyry systems. Published: 2026-05-28T07:03:22+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.582 (consider)

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

Porphyry molybdenum (Mo) deposits are the world's primary source of Mo and form through multi-stage processes involving source melting, magmatic differentiation, and fluid exsolution that drives hydrothermal Mo mineralization. The transition from magmatic to hydrothermal conditions is a critical phase in these magmatic-hydrothermal systems, yet its primary features are often obscured by subsequent overprinting, hindering direct observation. To quantitatively reconstruct the magmatic-hydrothermal transition and elucidate Mo enrichment mechanisms, this study utilizes in-situ geochemical analyses of various biotite generations from the Chaijiagou porphyry deposit, northern North China Block (NCB). Based on quantitative geochemical criteria, three biotite types were identified: early-stage magmatic biotite (Bt1), late-stage magmatic biotite (Bt2), and hydrothermal biotite (Bt3). Magmatic biotite shows a pseudo-A-type geochemical signature, indicating an extensional geodynamic setting related to lithospheric thinning of the NCB. Progressive melt evolution through fractional crystallization is evidenced by systematic decreases in K/Rb ratios from Bt1 (76.8–281.1) to Bt2 (63.8–116.9), accompanied by enrichment in incompatible elements. During the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution, hydrothermal biotite (Bt3) records a shift toward high F fugacity, with IV(F) increasing to 1.66–1.74, IV(F/Cl) rising to 6.24–6.44, and IV(Cl) decreasing to −4.71 to −4.56. item.title: Geochemical and petrogenetic characteristics of Miocene adakite-like rocks in Kashan, Central Iran: Implications for post-collisional crustal evolution
item.dc:creator: Tayebeh Khaksar
Ali Reza Davoudian
Shu-Guang Song
Nahid Shabanian
item.description: Early to Middle Miocene volcanic rocks in southern Kashan, part of the central Urumieh–Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), offer insights into adakitic-like magmatism and post-collisional crustal evolution in Iran. The suite mainly consists of andesite and rhyodacite with medium- to high-K calc-alkaline affinities, forming lava flows and domes. Zircon U–Pb dating gave ages of 18.2 ± 0.5 Ma for andesite and 14.9 ± 0.3 Ma for rhyodacite, indicating ongoing magmatic activity. These rocks exhibit characteristic adakitic geochemistry, including high Sr/Y (56–99) and La/Yb (20–72), low Y (5–13 ppm) and Yb (0.31–1.52 ppm), and no significant negative Eu anomalies. Sr–Nd isotopes ( 87 Sr/ 86 Sr = 0.7051–0.7062; εNd = −0.27 to −2.15) suggest a source close to bulk Earth composition. Enrichment in LILE and LREE, depletion in HREE, and trace element ratios (high Rb/Sr, high La/Sm, low Nb/U) indicate derivation from partial melting of a thickened mafic lower crust rather than a subducted slab. Geochemical and isotopic data imply progressive crustal thickening to approximately 40–50 km during the Early Cenozoic, linked to the Arabia–Eurasia collision. Slab break-off likely triggered asthenospheric upwelling, providing the heat necessary for lower crustal melting. The resulting magmas underwent minor fractional crystallization and limited crustal assimilation. These findings underscore the role of post-collisional geodynamics in producing adakitic-like magmas and improve understanding of crustal thickening and lithospheric evolution in continental collision zones. Published: 2026-05-27T07:00:44+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.581 (consider)
